narrative_overview:
The Hanifen family collection tells the story of three brothers — Bob (Robert), Jim (James Jr.), and John — from Des Moines, Iowa, as they navigate the upheavals of World War II, the Korean War, and the slow return to civilian life in postwar America. At the center is Bob Hanifen, whose letters span from his first weeks of military training in 1944–1945 through his post-WWII occupation duty in Italy, his discharge and resettlement in Dallas, Texas, and his ongoing correspondence with family back in Iowa well into the 1970s. The collection opens on a family already embedded in American working-class life: James Hanifen Sr. runs Hanifen Body & Paint Co. at 904 Keo Way in Des Moines, and by the summer of 1944 all three sons are swept into military service by the demands of WWII.

Bob's wartime letters form the richest vein of the collection. From basic training at Sheppard Field, Texas and the University of Wyoming's ASTP program, through technical training at Chanute Field, Illinois and then deployment to Italy in early 1946, Bob's letters to his father are a vivid, unfiltered record of a young Midwestern man encountering the wider world. He writes about B-29s and carbine rifles, about Italian women and German POWs, about the thrill of his first cross-country flight and the agony of wisdom teeth pulled without anesthesia. His voice is unmistakably his own — colloquial, affectionate, occasionally crude, always honest — and it never quite loses its wonder at the strangeness of military life abroad.

The Korean War chapter, told primarily through letters to and about John Hanifen, adds a darker register. John serves with the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion, a unit that performed extraordinary feats of military engineering — including what was officially recognized as the highest railroad bridge repair in the history of military engineering. He is at times in real danger (Communist guerrilla attacks near Pusan), subject to military discipline (a court martial reduces his grade in mid-1952), and dependent on letters from home and Dallas for connection to family life. Bob writes to him from Dallas with a mixture of brotherly teasing, genuine concern, and practical encouragement — urging him to make Sergeant, to use the GI Bill, to come home for Christmas.

Underlying all the military correspondence is the family anchor: James Hanifen Sr., the father, who writes in pencil on body shop stationery about elk hunting, pheasant season, Republican conventions, steel strikes, and the slow business of winter. His letters are brief, folksy, and deeply affectionate, and they root the entire collection in the texture of postwar Des Moines. By the 1950s and 1960s, the brothers are dispersed — Bob in Dallas, John rotating through Korea, Jim apparently back in Iowa — and the letters increasingly carry the weight of separation: Christmas hopes, debt repayments, photographs of children the uncles haven't met. The 1976 letter from Aunt Pauline, with its hair loss and calculators and dead relatives and newborn grandchildren, closes the arc quietly: the family has survived war and dispersal, and is now simply aging.

life_chapters.chapter_title: Before the Storm: Home Front and Early Military Life (1941–1944)
life_chapters.description: This chapter encompasses the Hanifen family's pre-deployment years and the earliest phase of Bob's military service. The documentary record here is sparse but telling: James Hanifen Sr.'s 1941 driver's license and the WWII gasoline ration book establish the family's grounded Des Moines existence — a working-class auto body shop owner, a 1941 Plymouth, wartime rationing. Bob graduates from high school around June 1944 (Letter 19 is a thank-you note for graduation gifts) and is already contemplating college or the Navy Air Corps. His brother Jim Jr. is stationed at Fort Riley, Kansas, in the Cook and Baker School. The family is intact but about to scatter.
life_chapters.description_continued: Letter 16, a July 1944 note from Dad to Jim Jr., captures the father's anxious pragmatism as Bob enters the ASTP program at the University of Wyoming — receiving no pay, needing soap and towels sent from home, on a grueling 6 AM to 10:30 PM schedule. The University of Wyoming letters (Letters 7, 18a, 65, 66, 49, 75) show Bob in a military-academic hybrid existence: room inspections, obstacle courses, basketball games, typhoid shots, and a girl who promises chicken dinner twenty miles out. The ASTP experience is both formative and temporary; when it winds down, Bob will flow into the standard AAF training pipeline.
key_events: Bob's high school graduation, June 1944
Jim Jr. stationed at Fort Riley, Kansas (Cook and Baker School)
Bob enters ASTP program at University of Wyoming, Laramie
WWII gasoline rationing in effect for James Hanifen Sr.
Iowa driver's license renewal, June 1941

life_chapters.chapter_title: Basic Training and the War's Final Year (Spring 1944 – August 1945)
life_chapters.description: This is the largest and most kinetic chapter of Bob's wartime experience, documented through dozens of letters from Sheppard Field, Texas, Chanute Field, Illinois, Fort Leavenworth, Kansas, and Greensboro, North Carolina. Bob cycles through basic training, weapons qualification (Marksman with carbine and .45), gas chamber exercises, bivouac, and endless reassignments as the AAF struggles to match training pipelines to the war's shifting needs. His frustration at being reshuffled from teletype operator to sheet metal worker to 'Air Corps unassigned' mirrors the broader chaos of mid-1945 mobilization. Throughout, his letters to his father are warm and detailed — the food, the weather, the buddies, the girls — and deeply revealing of a young man maturing under pressure.
life_chapters.description_continued: The most historically charged moment in the entire collection arrives in Letters 28 and 45: Bob writes on August 9–10, 1945 from Chanute Field referencing 'this new bomb' and then the next day reports that 'the Japs want to surrender.' He is almost certainly writing about the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, and his response is characteristically practical — he hopes it gets him home faster, but worries he will be assigned two years in 'the Islands' as occupation army. The war ends with Bob still at Chanute Field, finishing a sheet metal course that graduates September 22nd, meaning he will miss his birthday, Thanksgiving, and Christmas at home.
themes: WWII military training and pipeline confusion
soldier's daily life — food, weather, buddies, girls
anticipation of homecoming vs. fear of Pacific deployment
key_events: Basic training at Sheppard Field, Texas (spring 1944 and spring/summer 1945)
ASTP/training at University of Wyoming (1944)
Technical training at Chanute Field, Illinois (summer 1945)
Processing at Fort Leavenworth and Greensboro, NC
President Roosevelt's death, April 12, 1945 (Letter 46)
Atomic bombing of Japan and surrender, August 1945 (Letters 28, 45)
V-J Day, August 15, 1945 (referenced in Letters 28, 70)
Cross-country flight to St. Louis in C-47 (Letters 53, 54)

life_chapters.chapter_title: Post-War Italy: Occupation, Observation, and Coming Home (January – Late 1946)
life_chapters.description: After processing at Greensboro and Camp Kilmer, Bob ships out to Naples in January 1946 aboard the SS NYU Victory (Letter 84), arriving February 19. The Italy letters (Letters 3, 4, 6, 8, 11, 15, 18, 20, 22, 23, 24, 25, 39, 73, 80) form the richest sustained portrait of Bob abroad. Stationed first near Naples and Caserta, then traveling to Foggia, Bari, and Manfredonia for inspections, Bob serves as an ordnance inspector in a motorized unit — a job that suits him well and earns him praise from his lieutenant. He sends home detailed, unfiltered observations of post-war Italy: bomb damage, German POW labor, the barter economy of cigarettes, Italian women, the Red Cross, displaced persons fleeing Yugoslavia, a riot in Naples that kills fourteen people. He is simultaneously a tourist and an occupier.
life_chapters.description_continued: The Italy letters also show Bob's financial responsibility: he sends home almost all his pay in money orders, building up war bonds and savings for his return. His requests home are modest — 620 film, swimming trunks, the sports pages from the Drake Relays — and his affection for his father ('Lots of Love Your Son, Bobby') is unwavering. Jim Jr.'s February 1946 letter from Bethany, Missouri (Letter 2) adds a parallel thread: Jim is also processing out of service, emotionally raw after a car incident he blames himself for, in a letter that captures postwar disorientation in miniature. By mid-1946, Bob appears to be winding down his Italy posting and anticipating his return to the United States, though the precise discharge date is not documented in the collection.
themes: American GI as observer of European ruin
key_events: Bob departs Greensboro for Camp Kilmer, January 29, 1946
Voyage aboard SS NYU Victory, New York to Naples via Casablanca, February 8–19, 1946
Arrival in Naples, February 20, 1946
Assignment to ordnance inspection unit near Caserta
Inspection tours to Foggia, Bari, Manfredonia
German POW strike and armed guard duty, June 1946
Naples riot killing 14, June 1946
Jim Jr.'s car incident, Bethany, MO, February 1946

life_chapters.chapter_title: Civilian Reinvention: Bob in Dallas and the Korean War at Home (1946–1952)
life_chapters.description: After discharge, Bob Hanifen makes his way to Dallas, Texas rather than returning permanently to Des Moines. By 1951–1952 he is living at 3614 Noble Street, Dallas, working first at The Texas Company (Texaco) as a salesman earning $206.50/month, later at United States Fidelity and Guaranty Co. He is married to Jan (or close to marriage — she signs herself 'Your sister, almost' in November 1951). The Dallas letters reveal a man still finding his financial footing: asking to repay debts, comparing insurance rates, getting his car's valves ground, receiving income tax refunds and keeping only the 49 cents. The humor is self-deprecating but the underlying anxiety about money is real.
life_chapters.description_continued: Meanwhile, brother John is deployed to Korea with the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion, and the collection's Korean War materials — the Appreciation Letter from Lt. Colonel Miller, the court martial order, Bob's and Dad's letters to John — create a striking counterpoint to Bob's civilian struggles. Bob writes to John with brotherly affection and envy: John is earning combat pay and Corps of Engineers glory while Bob is selling Havoline oil in a Dallas drought. The Warnock estate letters (Letter 17) from 1954 provide a brief window into the extended family's legal and financial affairs, confirming that the three Hanifen brothers are heirs of Ollie Miller, connecting them to a broader Iowa farm family network.
themes: Dallas vs. Des Moines — geography of belonging
key_events: Bob settles in Dallas, working at Texaco by early 1952
Bob marries Jan (documented by late 1951)
John deploys to Korea with 439th Engineer Construction Battalion
439th's historic reconstruction of KIRA-CHON railroad bridge, April 1951
John's court martial, July 1952 (grade reduced, pay forfeited)
Korean War armistice negotiations ongoing throughout 1952
Warnock estate settlement, 1954 — Hanifen brothers as heirs of Ollie Miller

life_chapters.chapter_title: Aging and Memory: The Family in Later Decades (1954–1976)
life_chapters.description: The final chapter is slender but resonant. The collection's latest item is Aunt Pauline's 1976 letter (Letter 9) to Bob — a chatty, affectionate note about hair loss from bone-degeneration medication, a sold car, the Barquist grandchildren turning 16 and 24, Ann's overdue second baby, and Marcus's ruined fishing nets. Edith is dead; Doris 'still acts like a nut'; Jimmy called when Edith died. Pauline signs off with a self-deprecating note about her handwriting. It is a letter from old age — warm, slightly bewildered, full of small losses and small mercies — and it closes the collection with the same familial intimacy that opened it thirty-five years earlier.
life_chapters.description_continued: Between the 1954 estate documents and the 1976 letter, the record is thin. But the arc is clear: the Hanifen brothers have survived two wars, dispersal across the country, financial struggles, and the ordinary attritions of middle age. Bob, the collection's central figure, has moved from a Des Moines kid writing home from Sheppard Field about gas mask drills to a middle-aged man in Dallas receiving keepsakes from an elderly aunt. The family's story is, in the end, a quintessentially American one — working-class Iowans scattered by history, held together by letters.
key_events: Warnock estate legal proceedings concluded, late 1954
Edith's death (referenced in Letter 9, pre-1976)
Aunt Pauline's letter, May 25, 1976
Ann's second baby overdue, 1976
Marcus's fishing net losses, 1976

date_estimates.reasoning: Letter is written on University of Wyoming 'Cowboy Joe' letterhead from Laramie. References a ration book (WWII rationing), military training schedule (P.T., Military hour, inspections), and a girlfriend 20 miles out with chicken dinner. Letter 16 (dated July 13, 1944) from Dad to Jim Jr. explicitly mentions Bob's ASTP address at University of Wyoming, Laramie — 'He starts off at 6 AM + goes right thru until 10:30 PM.' This matches Bob's description in Letter 7 exactly. Letter 18a (also Wyoming, multi-day letter starting Monday) references Frontier Days in Cheyenne (held annually in late July) and a September 30–October 10 leave, also consistent with 1944. Letter 7 mentions 'tomorrow we have English first hour, Math 2 hours, 1 hr. of P.T., 3 hr of Physics Lab, 1 hr. of Military' — matches ASTP curriculum. Dated July 10, which in 1944 falls on a Monday; the letter reads as written mid-week given the next-day schedule described. Estimated July 1944.

WWII patriotic Christmas card from 'Pvt. Robert Hanifen.' Bob held the rank of Private during 1944–early 1945 (he is PFC by February 1946 per Letters 8, 15, 25). The card's verse about postponed joys 'until we meet again 'neath freedom's light' is consistent with active wartime service. Bob was at Sheppard Field in spring/summer 1944 (Letter 71 postmarked May 25, likely 1944) and at University of Wyoming in summer 1944. He may have been home or on leave at Christmas 1944 before shipping to Greensboro in January 1945. Most likely Christmas 1944, possibly 1943 if he was already in some form of service, but 1944 is most probable given his Pvt. rank and active training timeline.

Letter mentions leave September 30 to October 10 and C-47 planes to Des Moines and Omaha from the airport at 'Ft. Warren.' References physical fitness scores and visiting MP quarters. Letter 18a (Wyoming, 1944) also mentions a leave 'the last of the 30th of Sept. to Oct. 12' and states 'If you want to come up then would be a time.' The dates are nearly identical, strongly suggesting this is the same leave period. Fort Warren (Cheyenne, Wyoming) is near Laramie, consistent with Bob being at University of Wyoming in 1944. This is likely page 2 of a letter written in September 1944 from Laramie/Cheyenne area. Estimated September 1944.

Envelope is postmarked June 11, 1944 per the letter metadata ('postmarked June 11, 1944'). Bob thanks Jim for a graduation gift and mentions plans for college or the Navy Air Corps. This aligns with Bob graduating from high school in June 1944. The letter is addressed to Jim at Cook & Baker School, Fort Riley, Kansas. Date is confirmed by postmark.

Letter is written on American Red Cross letterhead (page 3), references learning to drive a White Co. tractor-truck, mentions possible convoy to Germany, food rations being cut, promotion to Corporal (Bob is promoted to Cpl. by Letter 22 dated May 9, 1946 — envelope shows 'Cpl. Robert E. Hanifen'), and a letter from Jimmy about possible discharge in three weeks. Also references Drake Relay sports pages — the Drake Relays occur annually in late April in Des Moines. Letter mentions being in Europe/Italy still. The reference to Jimmy getting out 'in three weeks' aligns with Letters 11 (June 7, 1946) asking 'Is Jimmy out of the Army yet?' suggesting this letter predates Letter 11. Estimated April 1946, consistent with the Drake Relays reference and Bob still being a PFC about to be promoted to Corporal.

Bob describes arriving at a new military base after traveling from Leavenworth through Tulsa and Dallas/Ft. Worth. Letter 46 (postmarked April 15, 1945) was written from Fort Leavenworth and mentions shipping to Texas on Monday. Letter 21 (April 23, 1945 postmark) is from Sheppard Field, Texas. This letter describes arriving at a base after the Leavenworth-to-Texas journey, placing it between those two letters. The flooding referenced ('Flood water held us up all day') matches spring 1945 conditions. The barracks 'made for 48 men which has now a total of 94 men' is consistent with overcrowded wartime training bases. Estimated approximately April 18–21, 1945.

Letter is explicitly dated 'March 2' and the content — PFC Bob at Caserta, Italy; first letter received (from Jimmy); Red Cross dance in Caserta; Ike jacket just issued; inspecting 643 MP and 3090 fire fighters; Lt. going on vacation last week of March — is entirely consistent with Bob's Italy posting from February 1946 onward. Letter 3 (dated March 22, 1946) mentions the same inspection duties and Lt.'s Switzerland vacation. Letter 8 (February 28, 1946) mentions upcoming trips to Foggia and Bari and the Red Cross Club in Caserta. 'March 2' falls between these two confirmed dates. Year is 1946 with high confidence.

Bob describes being on a 'permanent garden detail' at an Army Air Forces base, waiting to start school on Monday, moving to another squadron, and needing a shoe stamp (wartime rationing). References payday around the 10th and holding $4 plus a $10 money order from Dad. The mention of starting school 'Mon' and garden detail are consistent with the period at Chanute Field before school started (school began July 2 per Letter 54, dated Sun. 0130, ca. June 24, 1945). The garden detail may have been the 'temporary work while waiting to get in school' referenced in Letter 27 (July 20, 1945: 'I don't work in the garden anymore; that was just temporary work while we were waiting to get in school'). This places Letter 29 just before school started, approximately late June to early July 1945. Estimated July 1945, possibly late June.

Bob arrives Thursday at 8:10 at a new base, school starts Monday, he was a teletype operator now made a sheet metal worker — consistent with the reassignment chaos documented in Letter 33 (June 18, 1945) from Sheppard Field: 'all Teletype operators would go to Chanute as Sheet Metal workers.' Letter 33 says all teletype operators are going to Chanute. Letter 29a says he arrived at a new base (smaller than Sheppard) after the Leavenworth-to-Texas journey. References to Scott Field (teletype operators transferred there) are consistent with June 1945 reassignment period. The mention of 12-week course and school starting Monday fits the Chanute Field timeline. Estimated June–July 1945, likely just before or at arrival at Chanute Field.

Letter is from Sheppard Field, Texas (confirmed by return address: 'AAF - BTC - BKS - Bk02, Sqd I-3706-ABG, Sheppard Field'). Bob has qualified as Marksman with a carbine and will fire the .45 the next day, is going on bivouac 'next two' (weeks), mentions transferring to Keesler Field around 'the first of June,' and mentions a letter from Jimmy in Hawaii. Letter 71 (May 25, 1944, per the Friday May 25 date and context) also mentions qualifying with the .45 pistol and 31 days of basic training — 'my 31 day of basic and I only have 4 more days to go.' Letter 36's content (carbine Marksman, about to fire .45, bivouac upcoming) is earlier in basic training than Letter 71 (which describes just returning from bivouac and firing the .45 for score). Letter 36 is therefore from earlier in May 1944. The mention of sending Jimmy a letter to Hawaii and wanting to go to Keesler around June 1 is consistent. Estimated May 1944, approximately mid-May.

Letter is dated 'Sat. Morn. 9/10' with return address at Sheppard Field, Texas. Content describes post-WWII military life at Sheppard Field (June 1946 per metadata: Bob is still in the Army after the war's end). The discharge orders going to Denver are consistent with June 1946 demobilization. The 105° heat, 7½-mile hike, and dramatic storm match the metadata estimate of June 8, 1946. The '9/10' is likely the time (9:10 AM) not a date. Return address shows 'Pvt. Bob Hanifen, Sq. I-3716 ABU, Sheppard Field' — consistent with Bob back at Sheppard Field in 1946 after returning from Italy or being reassigned.

Letter is dated 'May 9, 1946' explicitly in the text ('Well this is the 1 of May' — likely a writing error for 'the 9th of May' given the date heading). Written on American Red Cross stationery, consistent with Bob's Italy posting. References Foggia (a major Italian air base Bob visited per Letters 18, 73, etc.), Jack Buckley, and the jeep — all consistent with Bob's Italy letters. The $59 money order referenced matches Letter 24 (June 19, 1946) which also mentions enclosing a $59 money order; however, the content differences suggest these are separate money orders at different times. The swimming trunks request and trip to Foggia with the Lt. are referenced in Letter 39 (May 9, 1946 per the date heading), while Letter 22 (also May 9, 1946) was sent separately. Both are from Caserta/Italy, May 1946. Confirmed as May 9, 1946.

Bob mentions mental classification tests, upcoming flight physical and psychomotor tests, gas mask day, Drake Relay results (held late April in Des Moines), Mother's Day flowers sent to grandmother, and word from Jimmy that 'San Francisco, Calif. That's the gateway to the Pacific. That is where we are going I guess. Seeing that Germany is almost over with.' V-E Day was May 8, 1945; 'Germany is almost over' suggests this was written in late April or very early May 1945, before the German surrender. The Drake Relays and Mother's Day both fall in May. This fits within Bob's Sheppard Field training period, consistent with Letters 21 (April 23, 1945, from Sheppard Field) and 52 (June 1, 1945, also Sheppard Field). Estimated May 1945, likely first or second week.

Letter is explicitly dated 'Sun. 20, 6:15 PM' and the content — Sheppard Field, Texas; .45 pistol practice tomorrow; bivouac for 3 days; 18 weeks mechanic school then 6 weeks gunnery; basic over by June 1; Hawaii/Guam reference — all match the spring 1946 Sheppard Field posting. This is confirmed by the metadata normalizing to 1946-05-20. The letter mentions sending $30 home after payday, consistent with Bob's 1946 financial pattern.

Bob is in the infirmary with gastroenteritis, has a furlough rate ticket on the Union Pacific Challenger (a famous train running through Cheyenne), and is traveling to Ames, Iowa at 1:10 AM Sunday. He mentions G.I. and department finals and the risk of flunking out ('if I don't the government pays your way home'). References to a Captain, furlough tickets, and academic finals are consistent with the ASTP program at University of Wyoming, Laramie (near Cheyenne). The annotation 'Oct. 476' is likely a later date notation in an unknown hand, not the letter's date. Given that the University of Wyoming ASTP letters span summer–fall 1944, and finals would occur at end of term, and Letter 18a mentions a leave September 30–October 10, this letter likely predates that leave — placing it in late September 1944. Alternatively, the notation may reference October and the letter could be from early October 1944. Estimated September–October 1944.

Bob describes his second day of training with gas mask drill, Texas dust, and hopes to go swimming at Wichita Falls with a Class A pass — all consistent with basic training at Sheppard Field, Texas. The payday of about $14 and the mention of sending blue swimming trunks are consistent with early basic training (low pay grade). This matches the spring 1945 Sheppard Field period documented in Letters 21 (April 23, 1945) and 52 (June 1, 1945). The gas mask chamber exercise description is very similar to Letter 48 (May 15, 1946, also Sheppard Field) but different enough in detail to suggest a different visit. Given the $14 payday (lowest enlisted pay), this is more likely the 1945 training rather than 1946. Estimated May 1945.

Letter is dated 'Jun. 1900' (military time for 7:00 PM in June) with Chanute Field return address (postmark Rantoul, Ill.). Bob describes flying to St. Louis in a C-47 — the same flight described in Letter 54 (dated Sun. 0130, which by context is the night before the flight) and Letter 78 (dated Mon. 1900, describing KP after the flight). Letter 54 is the Sunday night before; Letter 53 is written the same day as the flight (a Monday), and Letter 78 is the following Monday evening after KP. The C-47 flight to Curtis Wright Airport near St. Louis is the connecting event. Letter 54 mentions school starting July 2, and the flight occurs before that. Estimated June 25, 1945 (Monday).

Letter is dated 'Sun. 0130' from Chanute Field. Bob says he is about to fly to St. Louis 'this afternoon' in a C-47. School starts July 2. This Sunday precedes the Letter 53 flight (Monday). The night-before-the-flight context and school starting date place this on approximately June 24, 1945 (Sunday before the June 25 flight). Consistent with the Chanute Field timeline and school start of July 2.

Bob describes his first day of incoming processing at Chanute Field, starting a 15-week (84-day) technical training course in sheet metal work, welding, radiator repair. He has received overseas shots and notes the Army needs B-29 repair men urgently. School was set to start July 2 per Letter 54 (June 24, 1945). This letter describes the first day of processing, which would be around July 2 or shortly after. The description matches the 'Sgt T-BKS-249' squadron designation seen in other Chanute letters from July 1945. Estimated early July 1945.

Although the letter is dated 'June 29,' the postmark is July 29, 1945 per the metadata. Bob's jaw is still swollen (wisdom tooth extracted in Letter 35, dated July 1945 per Rantoul postmark), he references Air Forces Day on August 30, and plans to come home Sunday August 4 — consistent with Letter 27 (July 20, 1945) also mentioning August 4 as the planned home visit. 'June 29' is almost certainly a writing error for 'July 29.' Confirmed as July 29, 1945.

Letter is dated 'Jan. 31' but the content — 2,300 soldiers being processed, heading to New York City, APO number assignment, a large camp with 8 telephone centers of 30 booths each, crossing on a ship — matches exactly the description in Letter 23 (January 29, 1946): '23 hundred of us on the boat. We are going Naples, Italy' departing from Camp Kilmer NJ. Letter 60 says 'We are going to process here today so we will be leaving here pretty soon. Hope we get to see New York City.' This is Camp Kilmer. The date 'Jan. 31' in the letter is close to Letter 23's January 29 date — likely written January 31 at Camp Kilmer before departure, consistent with the 48-hour staging described in Letter 23. Confirmed as approximately January 31, 1946.

Letter is dated 'Wed. 1750' from Sheppard Field, Texas, postmarked June 13, 1945 per metadata. Bob mentions men being discharged ('all tech school men must be out by the 16th'), an exhausting 10-12 mile hike, and his buddies Jack Dee Ray and John Hare. This is consistent with the June 1945 Sheppard Field period between Letters 52 (June 1) and 33 (June 18). Confirmed as June 13, 1945.

Bob writes from University of Wyoming on 'Cowboy Joe' letterhead about ROTC inspection, basketball, cold weather (-10°F), promotion to Platoon Sergeant, brother Jim at Fort Riley, and anticipation of Air Corps call-up. The mention of 48 hours needed for Sophomore status and currently having 51 hours after one more term suggests he is completing his first or second semester. Given that the ASTP program at Wyoming is documented from summer 1944 (Letter 16, July 1944), and Bob was 'almost a Sophomore' in Letter 66 ('I will be a sophomore when the next term ends'), this letter appears to be from a slightly later term than the summer letters — perhaps late fall 1943 or winter/spring 1944, before the confirmed summer 1944 ASTP period. Jim being at Fort Riley's 'Stables' (cavalry training) and the Air Corps deferment context suggest early 1944. Estimated January–February 1944.

Bob writes from University of Wyoming about needing money for Christmas cards, returning January 8, meeting Jigger McClare, becoming a sophomore next term, a Christmas present from Beverly Ratcliffe of Des Moines, and condolences about an uncle. The return date of January 8 is consistent with post-Christmas semester break. The mention of becoming a sophomore 'when the next term ends' suggests he is in an earlier semester than Letter 65 (where he has 51 credit hours toward sophomore). This letter appears to be from just before Christmas break, likely December 1943 or possibly December 1944. Given that the summer 1944 ASTP letters clearly exist and this letter has a different, more collegiate (non-ASTP) tone with no military training references, December 1943 seems more likely — before the ASTP phase. Low confidence due to significant letter damage and illegibility.

Letter is explicitly dated 'Jan. 18, 45' in the text. Content matches perfectly: Bob has completed military processing at Greensboro, NC (same Service Club heading as Letter 13 dated Jan. 21), received five shots and smallpox vaccination, and is awaiting deployment to a POE. Letter 13 (Jan. 21) and Letter 32 (Jan. 14) are from the same Greensboro location, creating a tight date cluster in January 1945. Confirmed as January 18, 1945.

Letter is explicitly dated 'Jan. 25' and written from Greensboro, NC on Southern Bell Telephone stationery. Bob mentions leaving 'by Tues.' (approximately January 28), having done KP and detail work, and sending clothing items home. This fits between Letter 32 (January 14, 1946 — still at Greensboro) and Letter 23 (January 29, 1946 — departing for Camp Kilmer). Confirmed as January 25, 1946.

Letter is dated 'Jan. 23' on Army Air Forces stationery. Bob mentions buying an Eversharp pen for $3, lying around doing nothing waiting to ship out (rumored departure on the 25th), visiting the YMCA with Picard. This fits the Greensboro timeline: Letter 32 (Jan. 14), Letter 72 (Jan. 23), Letter 68 (Jan. 25), Letter 23 (Jan. 29). The Picard reference matches 'Don Picard' mentioned in Letter 68, confirming the same location and period. Confirmed as January 23, 1946.

Letter is explicitly dated 'March 20, 1946' in the text (with an odd notation 'June: 1045' which appears to be military time notation for 10:45 AM, not a month). Bob mentions getting out Friday night and being home for Easter, shipping equipment home, and possibly going to Kessler Field or Sheppard Field, Texas. Easter 1946 fell on April 21. Getting out on a Friday before Easter and being home in time is consistent with mid-to-late March 1946. The mention of a basketball tournament in the letter and the weather 'fine today' fits late March in the South/Midwest. Confirmed as March 20, 1946.

Bob mentions a furlough policy for 18-year-olds under December 1 — getting a 14-day furlough after their current term — and discusses travel pay between home, Des Moines, and Leavenworth, then to 'basic right from here.' He is restricted but planning to go to Alvin (likely Alvin, Texas, near Sheppard Field or a similar Southern base). References a letter from Jimmy saying he 'hasn't been warm for two days' (cold weather, suggesting fall), warm weather currently. This fits the late summer/fall 1944 period at the University of Wyoming or a transition base. The travel pay from Des Moines to Leavenworth (used in Letter 18a for the Fort Leavenworth to Laramie movement) and the 14-day furlough policy for 18-year-olds aligns with the ASTP/Air Corps training context of 1944. Estimated September–October 1944.

Letter is dated 'Mon. 1900' from Chanute Field Service Club, addressed to Aunt Pauline and Gran. Bob mentions having KP from 2:30 AM to 1:00 PM today (Monday), the C-47 flight to St. Louis 'yesterday' (Sunday), and anticipating KP again tomorrow (Tuesday). The C-47 flight is the same event described in Letters 53 (Monday flight account) and 54 (Sunday night before). Therefore this Monday is the day of the flight return, approximately June 25, 1945. Confirmed as approximately June 25, 1945.

Bob mentions taking a flight physical, a Class A pass, 20 days remaining before 7 weeks of gunnery training, and a furlough after gunnery. He is sunbathing and received a letter from Aunt Bonnie and Kay. The mention of gunnery training and a furlough matches Bob's projected training timeline described in Letter 47 (May 20, 1946 from Sheppard Field: '18 weeks mechanic school then 6 weeks gunnery') — but since that is 1946 and this letter's tone and context (class A pass into 'the Big City') matches early training, this could be either the 1945 or 1946 Sheppard Field period. Given the flight physical (Letter 43, May 1945, also mentions upcoming flight physical) and the 20-days-before-gunnery timeline, this may be from Sheppard Field, spring 1945. Estimated May 1945, possibly spring 1946.

Letter is explicitly dated '1, March [year illegible]' and the return address on envelope is 'Robert C. Hanifen 17120605, 42 Ord & Mt. Co. AF, APO 528, Postmaster New York, N.Y.' — APO 528 is the Italy/Mediterranean theater, and Bob is described in the Italy letters from February through June 1946. The mention of 60 new men arriving, a planned trip to MacFarlane (also mentioned in Letters 4, 8 from early March 1946) being cancelled, and the overall Italy context confirms this is March 1, 1946. The accompanying Western Union receipt from March 28, 1945 is a separate document included in the same envelope. Confirmed as March 1, 1946.

Multi-day letter (Monday to Friday) from University of Wyoming, Laramie on 'Cowboy Joe' letterhead. References Frontier Days in Cheyenne (annual rodeo, last week of July), a September 30–October 12 leave, fishing with Alvin (who appears in Letter 31a from June 1945 in Laramie), physical training, and Pauline/John/Fran. The Frontier Days reference places the start of the letter around late July 1944. The mention of snow starting around September 16 and invitation to come before August for fishing aligns with summer 1944. The leave dates of Sept. 30–Oct. 12 are almost identical to Letter 14's leave of Sept. 30–Oct. 10, confirming the same period. Estimated August 1944.

character_profiles.name: Robert Edward Hanifen
character_profiles.role: Central figure of the collection; eldest or middle Hanifen brother; WWII veteran; insurance/oil company salesman in Dallas
character_profiles.description: Bob Hanifen is the heart of this collection. A Des Moines native who entered the Army Air Forces in 1944 fresh out of high school, he trained at Sheppard Field (Texas), the University of Wyoming (ASTP), Chanute Field (Illinois), and Fort Leavenworth before shipping to Italy in early 1946 as a PFC ordnance inspector with the 2012th Ordnance and Maintenance Company. His letters to his father are vivid, affectionate, and unfiltered — full of military slang, frank observations of Italian life and culture, financial reports, and requests for film, swimming trunks, and sports pages. After discharge he settled in Dallas, working for The Texas Company (Texaco) and later US Fidelity and Guaranty Co., married Jan, and maintained affectionate but geographically distant relationships with his father and brothers. His ASN was 17170600.

character_profiles.name: James Edward Hanifen Sr.
character_profiles.role: Father of Bob, Jim Jr., and John; proprietor of Hanifen Body & Paint Co., Des Moines, Iowa
character_profiles.description: James Hanifen Sr. is the family anchor. He ran Hanifen Body & Paint Co. at multiple addresses in Des Moines (424 Virginia Ave., then 801 Park Street, then 904 Keo Way). His 1941 driver's license lists him as a 'Shop Proprietier,' 5'9', 175 lbs, light hair, gray eyes, born approximately 1890s. He wrote brief, folksy letters to his sons in pencil on body shop stationery — covering hunting trips, the steel strike, Eisenhower's nomination, elk in Colorado, and the slow business of winter. He sent money orders to his sons overseas, worried about them being cheated when they returned, and celebrated Bob's birthday (November 11) and John's military promotions. His letters have a working-class pragmatism and dry humor that comes through even in brief notes.

character_profiles.name: James Edward Hanifen Jr.
character_profiles.role: Middle Hanifen brother; WWII veteran (stationed Hawaii/Pacific); later in Iowa managing family connections
character_profiles.description: Jim Jr. is a shadowy but recurring presence. He served in WWII — stationed in Hawaii (Letter 36) and possibly Pacific islands — and by early 1946 was processing out of service at Bethany, Missouri, writing a heartfelt remorseful letter to his father about a car incident. He was stationed at Fort Riley (Cook and Baker School) in 1944, then Camp San Luis Obispo, California (Letter 18). Post-war he appears to be back in Iowa, apparently involved with or near the family shop. His wife (or the woman named in letters) is occasionally mentioned; by 1951 Jan Hanifen's letter to John mentions Jim writing about managing the shop. He had children — Jimmy III and Rosemary are mentioned in Bob's 1952 letters. His ASN was 37693423 (Letter 18 envelope).

character_profiles.name: John R. Hanifen
character_profiles.role: Youngest Hanifen brother; Korean War veteran; 439th Engineer Construction Battalion
character_profiles.description: John Hanifen served with the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion in Korea (APO 301), rising to Corporal before a court martial in July 1952 reduced him to Private E-2 and forfeited $65/month for three months (Letter 41 court martial order). He served with Co. C of the 439th and participated in the battalion's extraordinary engineering feats including the KIRA-CHON bridge reconstruction and the Han River girder launches. Bob's letters to him are warm and encouraging, urging him to make Sergeant, use the GI Bill, and come home for Christmas. His ASN was 55046284 (US). He was in Korea through at least mid-1952 and appears to have rotated back to the US by late July 1952 (Letter 55 was forwarded to 1115 Park Ave., Des Moines).

character_profiles.name: Jan Hanifen
character_profiles.role: Bob Hanifen's wife; typed letter-writer; insurance company employee in Dallas
character_profiles.description: Jan is Bob's wife, referred to affectionately as 'High Again Jan' on the custom stationery heading in Letter 76. She worked at United States Fidelity and Guaranty Co. in Dallas under claims superintendent Mr. Chittenden. Her November 1951 typed letter to John is warm, humorous, and socially engaged — she offers to set John up with girlfriends, teases Bob for opening her mail to John, and shares family news including Jim's scheme to manage the shop. She appears to have been recently married to Bob at the time of writing (signs herself 'Your sister, almost').

character_profiles.name: Pauline F. Hanifen
character_profiles.role: Bob's aunt; family correspondent; keeper of family keepsakes
character_profiles.description: Pauline F. Hanifen is Bob's aunt (probably his father's sister or sister-in-law). She writes to Bob in 1976 with a collection of 'treasures' (family keepsakes), reporting on her health troubles (bone degeneration medication causing severe hair loss), family birthdays, Ann's overdue baby, Marcus's ruined fishing nets, and various family phone calls. She is warm, self-deprecating about her handwriting, and clearly close to Bob's extended family. She is also mentioned in the Warnock estate Table of Heirship as 'Pauline Miller' — suggesting her birth name may be Pauline Miller, connecting her to the Miller family that is co-heirs of the Warnock estate.

character_profiles.name: Pearl Miller
character_profiles.role: Family member or close family associate; mentioned in 1976 letter
character_profiles.description: Pearl Miller is mentioned by Aunt Pauline in Letter 9 as a separate correspondent whose 'scribble' is better than Pauline's handwriting. Given the Warnock estate Table of Heirship (Letter 17) which lists 'Peal Miller' as an heir of Ollie Miller alongside Pauline Miller, Pearl Miller appears to be a sibling or close relative of Pauline, and thus part of the Miller branch of the extended Hanifen family.

character_profiles.name: Milo Olivier
character_profiles.role: Army buddy of John Hanifen; 443 Engineer Pipeline Company
character_profiles.description: Pfc Milo Olivier (US 550 678 92) of the 443 Engineer Pipeline Company at Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri, writes to John Hanifen in October 1951. He is crude, candid, and genuinely fond of John — frustrated with Army life and envious of friends who have been discharged. He got John's address from John's father during a delay en route home. His unit was originally slated to go to France as part of NATO buildup but the deployment was cancelled.

character_profiles.name: Lt. Davidson
character_profiles.role: Bob Hanifen's superior officer in Italy; former pilot; Ordnance Inspector's office
character_profiles.description: Lt. Davidson appears repeatedly in Bob's Italy letters as his direct superior — a 'swell guy' who lets Bob do inspections independently, goes on vacation to Switzerland, lets Bob drive the jeep, and praises Bob's inspection reports. He is a former pilot reassigned to ordnance inspection work, and his fair, collegial treatment of Bob is clearly appreciated. He is one of the positive figures in Bob's wartime experience.

character_profiles.name: M. C. Miller (Lt. Colonel)
character_profiles.role: Commanding officer of John Hanifen's Korean War unit
character_profiles.description: Lt. Colonel M. C. Miller, Corps of Engineers, commanded the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion during its 1951 Korean tour. His appreciation letter to John Hanifen is formal but genuinely admiring, documenting the battalion's extraordinary accomplishments in railroad bridge reconstruction, railroad rehabilitation, and mountain road improvement. He specifically credits John with contributions to the Han River girder launches and the Som River highway bridge — Korea's longest.

character_profiles.name: Jack Buckley
character_profiles.role: Friend/connection of Bob Hanifen in Italy; helped Bob get a camera and arranged outings
character_profiles.description: Jack Buckley is a recurring figure in Bob's Italy letters — a friend or connection who helps Bob get a camera, takes him and his lieutenant to wrestling matches in Foggia, and serves as a social connector in the post-war Italian posting. He is described as a 'sweet guy.' Bob visits him in Foggia on inspection trips.

character_profiles.name: Alvin Miller
character_profiles.role: Family friend in Laramie, Wyoming; fishing companion
character_profiles.description: Alvin Miller (return address 808 S. 2nd, Laramie, Wyoming) and his wife Carolyn are close family friends of the Hanifens. They correspond with Jim Hanifen Sr. and Jr., relay messages from Jimmie overseas, invite the family to visit for fishing, and by August 1945 have purchased a house in Laramie. Alvin appears in Letter 18a as someone Bob meets while at the University of Wyoming. Given the Warnock estate Table of Heirship (Letter 17) listing 'Alvin Miller' as an heir of Ollie Miller, Alvin is almost certainly a member of the Miller branch of the extended Hanifen family.

character_profiles.name: Edith
character_profiles.role: Family member; possibly Jim Sr.'s wife (Bob's mother) or a close family associate; deceased by 1976
character_profiles.description: Edith is mentioned repeatedly across the collection but never fully identified. She is mentioned in Letter 18a as a recipient of greetings ('Tell Fran & Pauline hello & John too'), in Letter 31a as the recipient of warm regards from Alvin and Carolyn Miller ('How is Edith?'), in Letter 61 (Bob buys her a present), and in Letter 9 (1976) where Aunt Pauline notes that 'young Jimmy called when Edith died.' The Miller family's warm regard for her and Jim Jr.'s centrality in Letter 31a suggest she may be Jim Jr.'s wife, but the pattern of Bob buying her a present and the family's collective references to her also support her being the mother/wife of James Sr. Her death, mourned in the 1976 letter, is a significant family loss.

recurring_themes.description: Despite being scattered across the country and the world by military service, the Hanifen family maintains its cohesion through letters. Bob writes to his father almost weekly during active service; Jim Jr. writes a raw confession after a personal failure; John receives letters from his father, brother, and friends throughout his Korea service. The Des Moines home — the body shop on Keo Way, the car in the garage, the pheasant hunting on Sunday — serves as a constant reference point across the collection. Even in the 1976 letter, Aunt Pauline is calling family members and tracking birthdays. The letters themselves are the family.

Financial anxiety runs through the collection like a thread. Bob sends almost every dollar home from Italy; he later struggles to repay debts to Jim in the early 1950s; Dad advises John to 'hold onto your dough' as soldiers come home and get cheated; the Warnock estate letter is a study in the small inheritances that matter enormously to working-class families. Bob's income ($206.50/month at Texaco in 1952), his $137.49 tax refund, his 20-cent insurance premium increase — all are carefully noted. The family's relationship to money is neither complaint nor pride but practical accounting.

recurring_themes.theme: Military Training and the Making of American Men
recurring_themes.description: The collection is, among other things, an extraordinary record of what it meant to be processed by the American military machine in 1944–1946. Gas chamber exercises, carbine qualification, bivouacs in 105° heat, wisdom teeth pulled at the base dentist, C-47 rides to St. Louis, German POWs doing all the work — Bob records it all with the attentive eye of someone trying to make sense of a world he has been suddenly dropped into. The training pipeline is chaotic (teletype to sheet metal to 'unassigned'), the Army is simultaneously terrifying and ridiculous, and Bob navigates it with humor and letters home.

Bob's Italy letters constitute a sustained amateur ethnography of post-war occupied Europe. He observes bomb damage, Italian civilians, German POW labor, the barter economy of cigarettes, displaced persons, the Naples riot, the cheap cost of getting anything done for a pack of Luckies. His observations are frank, sometimes crude, always vivid. He is simultaneously sympathetic (the Austrian refugee woman, the German soldiers who are 'swell guys') and unreflective about the politics of occupation. The Italy chapter is the richest sustained writing in the collection.

The three Hanifen brothers — Bob, Jim, John — are always present in each other's letters even when not the primary correspondent. Bob writes to Jim about Italian girls and asks him to send cigarettes; Bob writes to John about making Sergeant and using the GI Bill; John receives letters from Bob, Dad, Milo Olivier, and George and Jan; Jim writes to his father about failing a car test. The brotherly voice is distinctive: teasing, practical, affectionate, never sentimental. When Bob calls John 'Fratello' (Italian for brother) in his April 1946 letter, he means it.

recurring_themes.theme: Cars, Bodies, and the Family Trade
recurring_themes.description: The Hanifen Body & Paint Company is not just a business address — it is the family's identity. Letters are addressed to the shop. Dad's stationery bears the shop's name. Bob's ration book envelope advertises the shop. Jim Jr.'s 1946 letter is about a car incident that devastated him because he wanted to prove himself capable. Bob writes from Dallas about his 46 Chrysler, his insurance rates, getting his valves ground. The car is always there — as vehicle, as metaphor, as the thing the family fixes. Even John's car in Des Moines rises in value during the steel strike.

recurring_themes.theme: War's Reach and the Ordinary Soldier
recurring_themes.description: The collection spans from the home-front rationing of WWII through the Korean War and into the aftermath of both. Bob is present at one of history's pivotal moments — the day Japan sought to surrender — and his response is characteristically practical: 'this new bomb might get me home a little quicker.' John serves in a unit that performs feats of military engineering that are officially recognized as historic. Yet neither brother writes as a historian; they write as men who want to come home, see their families, drive their cars, and stop eating Army food. The gap between history's scale and the soldier's perspective is one of the collection's most poignant dimensions.

historical_events_referenced.event: World War II — Army Air Forces Training Pipeline
historical_events_referenced.how_it_affected_family: All three Hanifen brothers were drawn into military service during WWII. Bob went through the AAF training pipeline at Sheppard Field, University of Wyoming (ASTP), Chanute Field, and Greensboro, NC before deploying to Italy. Jim Jr. was stationed at Fort Riley (Cook and Baker School) and later Hawaii/Pacific. The family's correspondence from 1944–1945 is almost entirely defined by wartime military service.

Bob Hanifen served as an ordnance inspector in occupied Italy from February through at least mid-1946, as part of the U.S. post-war occupation forces. His Italy letters document German POW labor, Italian civilian conditions, early Cold War tensions ('the Russian situation'), and the chaotic but often fascinating experience of being a young American soldier in post-war Europe.

historical_events_referenced.event: Atomic Bombing of Japan and Japanese Surrender (V-J Day)
historical_events_referenced.how_it_affected_family: Bob wrote on August 9–10, 1945 referencing 'this new bomb' (Letter 28) and Japan's desire to surrender. Letter 70 (written August 14, the day of Japan's surrender) from family friends in Laramie, Wyoming notes 'by the looks of the news it will soon be all over with the Japs.' The war's end directly affected the family's calculus about when Bob would come home and whether he would be deployed to the Pacific for occupation duty — a fear he expressed in Letter 45.

Bob's demobilization process took months — processing at Greensboro, furlough extensions, deployment to Italy rather than discharge, and eventual return to civilian life in 1946. Special Orders Number 331 (Letter 10) extended his reenlistment furlough through January 1946. The chaotic, bureaucratic nature of demobilization is a recurring theme in his letters from late 1945 through 1946.

John Hanifen served in Korea with the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion (APO 301) from at least early 1951 through mid-1952. The Korean War separated John from his family for an extended period, prompted Bob's frustrated letters about draft board inequities, and produced John's court martial in July 1952. Dad's letters to John reflect home-front concern about soldiers being cheated upon return. The 439th's extraordinary engineering accomplishments (Letter 34) represent John's most significant historical contribution.

historical_events_referenced.event: Death of President Franklin D. Roosevelt
historical_events_referenced.how_it_affected_family: Bob mentions in his April 15, 1945 letter from Fort Leavenworth that there is no dance 'on account of the president's death' — a passing but historically significant reference to FDR's death three days earlier, which Bob notes he 'sure hated to hear about.'

historical_events_referenced.event: 1952 Republican National Convention and Eisenhower Nomination
historical_events_referenced.how_it_affected_family: Dad's July 12, 1952 letter to John references 'This is the Day, The Republicans & Eisenhower are Shouting' — capturing the 1952 Republican National Convention in real time and suggesting the family followed national politics with interest.

Dad's letter to John mentions the ongoing steel strike of 1952 and notes that John's car has risen in value as a result ('if the Strike Keeps up, it may go to 2000'). This reflects the strike's impact on used car prices due to reduced new car production.

historical_events_referenced.event: Early Cold War and Soviet Tensions
historical_events_referenced.how_it_affected_family: Bob's March 22, 1946 letter from Italy mentions 'This Russian situation don't look too good to me, as we are in the hot spot right here' — one of the earliest Cold War references in the collection, reflecting anxiety among U.S. occupation forces in Europe about Soviet intentions in the immediate post-WWII period.

James Hanifen Sr.'s 'A Basic Mileage Ration' gasoline ration book for his 1941 Plymouth documents the family's direct experience of wartime rationing on the home front. The 'A' card was the lowest-priority ration, restricting non-essential driving.

Bob Hanifen participated in the Army Specialized Training Reserve Program (ASTRP) at the University of Wyoming, Laramie, from approximately summer 1943 through 1944. The program gave him a rigorous academic and military education before he entered the main AAF training pipeline. Dad's letter (Letter 16) vividly describes the financial burden of supporting Bob through the program while he received no military pay.

chronological_order.one_line_summary: Iowa driver's license and temporary renewal permit for James E. Hanifen Sr., establishing his identity as a Des Moines auto shop proprietor in 1941.
WWII gasoline ration book for James Hanifen Sr.'s 1941 Plymouth, documenting wartime home-front rationing.
Bob writes from University of Wyoming near Christmas, mentioning needing money, a new girl named Jigger McClare, and becoming a sophomore next term.
Bob reports from University of Wyoming on a successful ROTC inspection, promotion to Platoon Sergeant, and Jim at Fort Riley.
Bob at Sheppard Field, Texas qualifies as Marksman with a carbine, notes Jimmy is in Hawaii, and expects transfer to Keesler Field in June.
Bob just returned from bivouac at Sheppard Field on day 31 of basic training, qualified Marksman with .45 pistol, and anticipates assignment to Keesler Field.
Bob thanks Jim for a graduation gift and announces plans for college or the Navy Air Corps, sharing family news from Des Moines.
Dad writes to Jim Jr. with Bob's ASTP address at University of Wyoming, describing Bob's grueling 6AM–10:30PM schedule and the financial burden of supporting him.
Bob writes from University of Wyoming describing his military training schedule, stomach illness from cantaloupe, and a planned Sunday visit to a girlfriend twenty miles out.
Multi-day letter from University of Wyoming detailing Bob's expenses, physical training, mountain hike to 8,360 feet, and plans to see Frontier Days in Cheyenne.
Page 2 of a letter describing a weekend at Fort Warren's MP quarters, a church visit, and airport arrangements for a September 30–October 10 leave home.
Bob explains furlough policies for 18-year-olds, discusses travel pay between Laramie, Des Moines, and Leavenworth, and asks about a missing raincoat.
Bob writes from the base infirmary after a gastroenteritis epidemic, shares good news about a furlough rate ticket on the Union Pacific Challenger to Ames, Iowa.
Bob writes from Fort Leavenworth about shipping to Texas Monday, mentions President Roosevelt's death, and asks about brother Jim.
Bob at Sheppard Field describes the B-29 gunner training pipeline and estimates he might be overseas by Christmas.
Bob describes arriving at Sheppard Field after a train journey through Tulsa and Dallas, finding crowded barracks designed for 48 men now holding 94.
Bob describes classification tests, flight physical, gas mask day, Mother's Day flowers to grandma, and learns San Francisco will be his gateway to the Pacific theater.
Bob's second day of training features gas mask exercises, Texas dust storms, and anticipation of swimming at Wichita Falls with a Class A pass.
Bob recounts a Memorial Day gas attack drill at Sheppard Field and describes awaiting classification orders while fellow soldiers are reassigned.
Envelope only from Sheppard Field, Texas, postmarked June 10, 1945, with a 2-cent postage due charge.
Bob at Sheppard Field describes men being discharged rapidly, an exhausting 12-mile hike, and his fondness for buddies Jack Dee Ray and John Hare.
Bob's deployment is postponed yet again due to poor railroad conditions; he is reshuffled from teletype operator to sheet metal to 'Air Corps unassigned' and expresses deep frustration.
Bob at Sheppard Field endures 100-degree heat, a broken-up flight unit, and a 7-mile hike while waiting for orders.
Bob on garden detail at an AAF base, restricted to area, waiting for school to start and struggling with a tough sergeant about passes.
Bob arrives at a new base, learns he has been reassigned from teletype operator to sheet metal worker, and plans weekend trips home.
Alvin and Carolyn Miller in Laramie relay Jimmie's request to send photo negatives to Jim and invite the family for a late July fishing vacation.
Bob writes at 1:30 AM from Chanute Field Service Club about a Sunday C-47 flight to St. Louis and marvels at B-29s on the field.
Bob writes to his aunt and grandmother from Chanute Field after KP duty, describing the C-47 flight to St. Louis and his 16-week training timeline.
Bob recounts the thrilling C-47 flight to St. Louis — 9,000 feet at 160 MPH — including a hydraulic fluid scare and the sight of the flooding Mississippi.
Bob's jaw is still swollen from dental work; he plans to come home August 4 and mentions Air Forces Day on August 30.
Bob at Chanute Field begins riveting training, mentions B-29s, plans a Chicago trip, and wonders if he might see Jimmy on a Pacific island.
Bob describes a harrowing dental visit to have two wisdom teeth surgically removed at Chanute Field and notes he is studying Plexiglas bullet hole repair.
Bob at Chanute Field has started riveting classes, plans to come home August 4, and asks about his war bonds.
Bob recounts a lively Chicago weekend with buddy Hank, meeting SPARS on the train, and outcompeting sailors at meeting girls.
Bob describes a Chicago weekend trip with Kay, visiting Aunt Bonnie in Blue Island, and missing his return train by five minutes.
Bob invites his father to drive to Chanute Field for Air Forces Day on August 30, and reports his jaw is still swollen after another tooth extraction.
Bob apologizes for not visiting Des Moines due to his C shift schedule, mentions a trip to Chicago, and celebrates Chanute Field's baseball win over Great Lakes.
Bob references 'this new bomb' on August 9 and the next day reports Japan wants to surrender, hoping it will get him home sooner.
Bob returns from a wonderful Peoria weekend with buddy Hank and expresses anxiety about potentially being assigned two years of Pacific occupation duty.

chronological_order.to: James Edward Hanifen (Jim Sr. or Jr.)
chronological_order.one_line_summary: Written the day of Japan's surrender, Alvin and Carolyn share family news and optimism that the war with Japan will soon be over.
Bob returns to Chanute Field from Chicago, reports a worn-out tire on the family Chevy, and asks about his brother Jimmy.
Bob hitchhikes back to Chanute Field through Moline and Peoria, reports his class graduates September 22, meaning he will miss birthday and holidays.
Bob reports a great visit home, safe return to Chanute Field, and plans to get Edith a present and come home again later that week.

chronological_order.from: Lt. Jon (USNR, USS Gen. C.G. Morton AP-138)
chronological_order.one_line_summary: A Navy lieutenant aboard a troop transport sends a box of cigars to James Hanifen Sr. in gratitude for past favors, sharing news of his wife Donnie in San Francisco and previous trips to Australia and India.

chronological_order.from: Headquarters AAF Overseas Replacement Depot, Greensboro, NC
chronological_order.to: Pvt Robert E. Hanifen, Des Moines, Iowa
chronological_order.one_line_summary: Official military orders extending Bob Hanifen's reenlistment furlough by 34 days, requiring him to return to Greensboro no later than January 7, 1946.
WWII-era patriotic Christmas card from Pvt. Bob Hanifen acknowledging wartime separation and affirming the spirit of Christmas.
PFC Bob at Greensboro, NC reports his unit is likely heading to Germany and expects to ship out in under two weeks.
Bob at Greensboro reports completing processing, receiving five shots and a smallpox vaccination, and awaiting departure for a Port of Embarkation.
Bob at Greensboro buys an Eversharp pen, idles waiting to ship out with a rumored departure date of the 25th.
Bob at Greensboro asks for an Esterbrook pen, mentions sending $90 for war bonds, and expects to reach his POE soon.
Bob at Greensboro describes KP and salvage detail duty, sends clothing home for Johnny and Herby, and expects to leave by Tuesday.
Bob departs Greensboro for Camp Kilmer, NJ with 2,300 soldiers, destination Naples, Italy.
Bob at Camp Kilmer reports being processed with 2,300 soldiers before shipping out, asking his father to get a duplicate car registration.
Ship's newspaper and voyage log from the SS NYU Victory document Bob's 11-day troopship journey from New York to Naples via Casablanca, February 8–19, 1946.
Bob arrives in Naples, vividly describes the bombed city, a Casablanca stopover, the barracks at Mussolini's former headquarters, and the chaotic post-war street life.
Jim Jr. writes a heartfelt, remorseful letter from Bethany, Missouri apologizing for screwing up a deal involving the car, feeling like crying despite being grown.
Bob describes his new role as ordnance inspector in Italy, inspecting a German POW motor pool, and colorful observations of post-war Italian life.
Bob inspects the Battalion Motor Pool, drives a 10-ton prime mover, meets Anna at the Red Cross in Caserta, and notes Naples is off-limits due to VD.
Brief letter from Bob at his Italy posting noting 60 new men arrived and a planned trip to MacFarlane was cancelled.
Bob at the Red Cross in Caserta reports his first letter received (from Jimmy), his Ike jacket, and praiseworthy inspection work.
Bob describes inspection duties, a possible trip to Rome and northern Italy, and expresses anxiety about 'the Russian situation' from his European posting.
Bob encloses checks totaling $99 from his base pay, asks for an inventory of his war bonds, and mentions visiting Jack Buckley in Foggia about his camera.
Bob in Caserta writes to brother Jim with colorful anecdotes about nearly getting married to an Italian girl, an inspection tour to Bari, and seeking a German Luger for protection.
Bob in Caserta encloses a $41 money order, mentions a Red Cross dance, and hopes to join a convoy to Germany on May 26.
Bob asks about war bonds and his $59 money order, requests his swimming trunks, and describes a wet drive back from wrestling matches in Foggia.
Bob at Sheppard Field describes upcoming .45 pistol practice, a three-day bivouac, and his assignment as an AM Gunner heading to mechanic school.
Bob in Italy writes to Jimmy about nearly making Sergeant, MP trouble over a missing button, flying to Foggia, and asking Dad to buy a Mercury or Ford.
Bob at Sheppard Field describes an Oklahoma storm, tear gas chamber training, KP duty, and laments the scarcity of women in Texas.
Bob describes a horrifying nearby accidental shooting, plans to send home a .45 pistol, and asks if Jimmy is out of the Army yet.
Bob at Sheppard Field describes a 105° hike, gas drills, and a violent storm that destroyed an outdoor theater and flooded the town.
Bob in Naples describes hauling equipment to the docks, guarding striking German POWs from a 60-foot tower, and driving through a 2,000-person crowd before a deadly Naples riot.
Bob learns to drive a 90-foot White Co. tractor-truck, hopes for a Germany convoy job, gets promoted to Corporal, and asks for Drake Relay sports pages.
Bob plans to take the train to Des Moines for Easter, ships his equipment home, and notes a possible reassignment to Kessler or Sheppard Field, Texas.
Bob describes taking a flight physical, sunbathing, and anticipating gunnery training after 20 more days, with a furlough to follow.

chronological_order.from: James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad) and George & Jan
chronological_order.one_line_summary: Dad shares hunting news and worry about Bob's struggling business; friends George and Jan announce a pregnancy and apartment house purchase.
Dad writes about a major early snowstorm, pheasant hunting plans, checks sent to John, and a warning that returning soldiers are being financially cheated.
Army buddy Milo Olivier writes John from Fort Leonard Wood, frustrated with Army life and reporting Gene De Young received his discharge.
Jan's warm letter offers to set John up with girlfriends and shares family news; Bob's letter asks if John got the Christmas package and voice recording.

chronological_order.from: Lt. Colonel M. C. Miller, 439th Engineer Construction Battalion
chronological_order.one_line_summary: Appreciation letter and Narrative Summary documenting the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion's extraordinary Korean War service, including the historic KIRA-CHON bridge reconstruction.
Bob in Dallas encourages John to earn Sergeant stripes, mentions his Texaco job at $206.50/month, and asks about the Christmas package.
Bob promises to repay his debt to Jim within two months, mentions his income tax refund of $137.49, and continues his job at The Texas Company.
Bob got his car's valves ground, asks about Jim's insurance carrier, acknowledges Jim has been helping with a car payment, and asks for photos of the kids.
Dad writes about Eisenhower's Republican nomination, the steel strike, Father's Day gifts (Jimmie sent a shirt, Bob sent nothing), and advises John to hold onto his money.
Bob teases John about still being in Korea, shares the Dallas drought, encourages John to use the GI Bill for Iowa University, and encloses a Des Moines stock car racing clipping.
Bob in Dallas shares Indianapolis 500 news, expresses fury at the draft board system while John remains in Korea, and urges John to make Sergeant.
Bob invites Dad to Chanute Field for Air Forces Day; a separately filed 1952 court martial order for Cpl. John Hanifen in Korea is stored with this letter.

chronological_order.to: Heirs of Frank J. Warnock, deceased
chronological_order.one_line_summary: Attorney letters and Table of Heirship documenting the Warnock estate settlement, revealing Robert, John, and James Hanifen as heirs of Ollie Miller.
Aunt Pauline sends Bob family keepsakes and updates on health problems, family birthdays, Ann's overdue baby, and Marcus's ruined fishing nets — the collection's final letter.
